label: Ezra-Nekhemyah — Part 1 — Ezra
summary_plain: Ezra records the return from Babylon after Koresh (Cyrus) of Persia issues his decree: YHWH has charged me to build him a house at Yerushalayim. The exiles who return are listed by family: the children of Yehudah, Binyamin, and the priests and Levites. The temple is rebuilt. Ezra the scribe arrives from Babylon with the Torah of Moshe in his hand. He discovers that the returned exiles have intermarried with the peoples of the land and calls the assembly to separation — to put away the foreign wives and return to the covenant.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads Ezra from DSS witnesses (4QEzra, fragmentary) and the Orit Ge'ez. The return from Babylon is critical for understanding post-exilic Israelite identity. Only a fraction of the exiled population returned — primarily from Yehudah (Judah), Binyamin (Benjamin), and Levi. The northern tribes — exiled by Assyria in 722 BCE — did not return. They were already scattered across the Assyrian Empire's territory and beyond, into Africa, Central Asia, and regions the text does not track. The term 'Jew' (Yehudi) originates in this period — it means 'from Yehudah,' one of twelve tribes. The Great Eraser mechanism collapses all twelve tribes into 'Jews,' erasing the ten northern tribes and their descendants entirely. Ezra's genealogy lists (chapters 2 and 8) record only southern tribal families. The Koresh decree (1:1-4) is corroborated by the Cyrus Cylinder (539 BCE, British Museum) — the Persian imperial policy of restoring displaced peoples to their homelands.

key_terms.definition: King of Persia who conquered Babylon in 539 BCE and issued the decree permitting the Judean exiles to return to Yerushalayim and rebuild the temple. Called 'YHWH's anointed' in Yeshayahu 45:1 — a non-Israelite ruler used by YHWH. Anglicized as Cyrus.

A person from the tribe of Yehudah or the territory of Yehudah. The word 'Jew' derives from Yehudi. It originally designated one tribal-territorial identity — not all twelve tribes of Yisra'EL. The Great Eraser collapsed all Israelite identity into this one term.

Ezra is called 'a scribe skilled in the Torah of Moshe' (7:6). The sofer preserves, copies, and teaches the Torah. Ezra's scribal activity in the 5th century BCE is a key moment in Torah transmission — but the Torah itself predates Ezra by centuries.

fill_blank.prompt: Thus says Koresh king of Persia: YHWH the Creator of heaven has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and he has charged me to build him a house at ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: Thus says Koresh king of Persia: YHWH, the Creator of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and he has charged me to build him a house at Yerushalayim, which is in Yehudah.

fill_blank.prompt: This Ezra went up from Babylon. He was a scribe skilled in the ______ of Moshe that YHWH the Creator of Yisra'EL had given.
fill_blank.source_quote: This Ezra went up from Babylon. He was a scribe skilled in the Torah of Moshe that YHWH the Creator of Yisra'EL had given.

fill_blank.prompt: Then rose up the heads of the fathers' houses of ______ and Binyamin, and the priests and the Levites.
fill_blank.source_quote: Then rose up the heads of the fathers' houses of Yehudah and Binyamin, and the priests and the Levites, everyone whose spirit the Creator had stirred to go up to rebuild the house of YHWH that is in Yerushalayim.

multiple_choice.question: Who issued the decree allowing the exiles to return to Yerushalayim?
multiple_choice.source_quote: In the first year of Koresh king of Persia... YHWH stirred up the spirit of Koresh king of Persia, so that he made a proclamation.

multiple_choice.question: Which tribes returned from Babylon?
options: Primarily Yehudah, Binyamin, and the Levites
multiple_choice.source_quote: Then rose up the heads of the fathers' houses of Yehudah and Binyamin, and the priests and the Levites.

multiple_choice.question: What did Ezra bring from Babylon?
options: The Torah of Moshe — he was a scribe skilled in it
multiple_choice.source_quote: He was a scribe skilled in the Torah of Moshe that YHWH the Creator of Yisra'EL had given.

faq.question: Why does it matter that only southern tribes returned from Babylon?
faq.answer: Ezra 1:5 is explicit: 'Then rose up the heads of the fathers' houses of Yehudah and Binyamin, and the priests and the Levites.' Three tribal groups. The ten northern tribes — Re'uven, Shim'on, Dan, Naftali, Gad, Asher, Yissakhar, Zevulun, Efrayim, and Menasheh — were exiled by Assyria in 722 BCE, 136 years before the Babylonian exile. They did not return with Ezra. They were already scattered — into Africa, Central Asia, and regions beyond the text's tracking. The word 'Jew' (Yehudi) derives from Yehudah — one tribe. The Great Eraser mechanism collapsed all twelve tribes into 'Jews,' erasing the majority of Yisra'EL from the historical record. When modern institutions say 'Jewish people,' they reference the post-exilic Yehudah community. The ten tribes and their descendants — the majority of Yisra'EL — are not accounted for. They did not disappear. They were erased from the narrative.

faq.question: What does the Koresh (Cyrus) decree reveal about YHWH's sovereignty?
faq.answer: Koresh is a Persian king — not an Israelite. Yet YHWH uses him to restore the exiles to Yerushalayim. Yeshayahu 45:1 calls Koresh 'YHWH's anointed' (mashiach) — the same title given to Israelite kings. YHWH's sovereignty operates through non-Israelite rulers when it serves the covenant purpose. The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BCE, British Museum) corroborates the decree — it records Koresh's policy of restoring displaced peoples to their homelands and their temples. The cylinder does not mention YHWH by name — it attributes the policy to Marduk. But the Hebrew text attributes the same policy to YHWH. Both sources describe the same historical event from their own theological framework. The primary sources agree on the event; they differ on the attribution.

faq.question: What happened to the ten northern tribes?
faq.answer: The Assyrian Empire exiled the northern kingdom of Yisra'EL in 722 BCE (2 Melakhim 17). Assyrian policy was population displacement — scattering conquered peoples across the empire to prevent rebellion. The ten tribes were dispersed into Assyria, Media, and beyond. They did not return with the Judean exiles under Koresh. Traditions across Africa — the Igbo of Nigeria, the Lemba of Southern Africa, the Beta Israel of Ethiopia — preserve Israelite practices, names, and covenant identity consistent with northern tribal origins. DNA studies (Parfitt, 2000; Behar, 2010) have confirmed Levantine genetic markers in these populations. The Great Eraser treats these communities as curiosities or imposters rather than as the descendants of the majority of Yisra'EL. Ezra records who returned. It does not say the rest ceased to exist.
